title: When thoughts run out
text:
When thoughts run out
I step into the woods
and gather
shepherd's purse.

title: Like a small stream
text:
Like a small stream
through mossy stone,
I keep going quietly
until I turn clear.

title: At dusk I climb Kugami
text:
At dusk I climb Kugami.
A deer calls.
Its voice sinks
into fallen maple leaves
at the mountain's foot.

title: How could I sleep in this moonlight?
text:
How could I sleep in this moonlight?
Friends, come.
Let us sing and dance
until dawn.

title: A little drunk
text:
A little drunk,
lying under the huge sky,
I dream beautifully
beneath cherry blossoms.

title: Wild roses from the field
text:
Wild roses from the field,
frogs calling everywhere.
Float the petals in wine.
Enjoy this brief hour.

title: Spring will open every branch
text:
Spring will open every branch.
Flowers will return.
But the children
who fell like last autumn leaves
will not come back.

title: The wind is still
text:
The wind is still.
Blossoms are gone.
Birdsong deepens as mountains darken.
This, too, is the Dharma.

title: The thief left one thing behind
text:
The thief left one thing behind:
the moon
at my window.

title: The wind gives me enough fallen leaves
text:
The wind gives me enough fallen leaves
to keep a small fire going.

title: After many years away
text:
After many years away,
I return to my village,
sick,
and sleep in an inn.

One robe.
One bowl.
I burn incense
and sit through the rain.

All night the drizzle keeps falling.
Inside the dark room,
old journeys come back one by one.

title: An old grave at the foot of a quiet hill
text:
An old grave at the foot of a quiet hill,
weeds high,
no one tending it.
Only a woodcutter passes sometimes.

I was his student once,
a rough-haired boy by the Narrow River.
Then I left,
and years went silent between us.

Now I pour clear water on his stone
and bow.
The sun drops behind the ridge,
wind fills the pines,
and I cannot walk away.
My sleeves are wet with tears.

title: When I was young
text:
When I was young
I put books aside
and wanted to be a saint.
I wandered many springs as a beggar monk.

At last I came home
and built a hut under a rough cliff.
Birdsong became my music.
Clouds became my neighbors.

Below: a clear spring.
Above: pines and oaks for shade and firewood.
Day after day,
free,
I have no wish to leave.

title: Yes, I am a fool
text:
Yes, I am a fool,
living among trees and grass.
Do not ask me about illusion or enlightenment.
I only smile to myself.

I wade streams on thin legs,
carry my bag through spring weather,
and keep walking.
The world owes me nothing.

title: Snow blends with wind
text:
Snow blends with wind.
Wind blends with snow.

By the hearth I stretch my legs,
doing nothing,
kept inside this hut.

Counting days,
I see even February
has already passed,
like a dream.

title: No luck on my begging rounds today
text:
No luck on my begging rounds today.
From village to village
I dragged this body.

By sunset,
mountains still stand between me and home.
The wind cuts through my robe.
My small bowl looks emptier than ever.

Still,
this is the path I chose:
cold,
hunger,
disappointment,
and a strange peace inside them.

title: I found this cracked wooden bowl in bamboo
text:
I found this cracked wooden bowl in bamboo,
washed it in spring water,
and mended it.

After meditation
it holds morning gruel.
At night,
soup or rice.

Worn,
split,
weathered,
out of shape—
still,
it is of noble stock.

title: In midsummer I walk with my staff
text:
In midsummer I walk with my staff.
Old farmers see me
and call me over for a drink.

We sit in the fields,
using leaves as plates.
Warm with sake,
happy,
I fall asleep
on the edge of a rice paddy.

title: I watch people in this world
text:
I watch people in this world
wear themselves out
chasing things.

Desire never fills.
Even when it does,
joy vanishes quickly,
and the grip tightens.

Like monkeys reaching for the moon in water,
they fall into whirlpools.
All night I think of them,
unable to stop these tears.

title: In this broken three-room hut
text:
In this broken three-room hut
I have grown old.
This winter is the hardest yet.

I sip thin gruel
and wait through the freezing night.
Will I last until spring?

I cannot go out to beg for rice.
How will I survive this cold?
Even meditation gives little warmth now.
So I write poems
for friends who have already gone.
